subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Fieldwork question is precisely defined with clear links to bid-rent theory, enabling hypothesis formation.
Introduction locates Abidjan and Plateau district specifically; maps identify transect routes and individual sites, demonstrating feasibility.
Three separate maps follow cartographic conventions (north arrows, legends, scale bars) and focus on the study area.
weaknesses: Figure 2 lacks a north arrow and scale bar, limiting spatial clarity.
Scope could be sharpened by specifying primary urban stress indicators (e.g. traffic flow vs. pedestrian counts).
Socio-economic context (income distribution, land use) could be deepened for richer geographic framing.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the descriptor for the top band: the question is geographical with clear theoretical links, the location is precise and feasible for primary data collection, and mapping conventions are correctly followed. Minor cartographic omissions and contextual depth suggestions do not detract from a fully developed, syllabus-linked question and context.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods are clearly described and each linked to the underlying bid-rent theory and hypotheses.
Systematic sampling at 100 m intervals, decibel readings and bipolar EQS provide a coherent methodological framework.
Implementation (3-minute counts, averaging dB, paired EQS) produced a consistent primary dataset across 40 sites.
weaknesses: Justification for the 40-site sample size is not provided, leaving representativeness unexplained.
The validity and calibration of the bipolar EQS scale require further elaboration to ensure consistency.
The theoretical rationale for choosing noise and EQS as proxies could be strengthened with deeper grounding in bid-rent literature.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While strand assessments initially placed the work in band 2, the methods are described with sufficient detail and clear relevance to each hypothesis, and implementation yielded reliable primary data. A fuller theoretical justification and sample-size rationale would strengthen the section, but the alignment with research questions and coherent execution support awarding the top mark for Criterion B.

strengths: A substantial, directly relevant primary dataset (40 sites on four transects for four variables) supports full analysis.
Presentation techniques (tables, annotated photographs, scatter plots with trend lines and RÂ²) are effective and clearly interpreted.
Figures generally adhere to conventions (titles, labelled axes, legends, scale bars, north arrows) with only very occasional lapses.
weaknesses: The method of aggregating EQS sub-indicators into a single index is not fully justified.
Inconsistent heading formats and occasional truncated captions distract from otherwise polished tables and figures.
Some graph axes omit units (e.g. âmâ for distance), which should be standardized across all presentations.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although minor formatting and explanatory issues exist, the data are entirely relevant and sufficient, and presentation techniques are among the most effective for conveying analysis. Full adherence to conventionsâwith only infrequent, non-critical errorsâwarrants the highest mark in Criterion C.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Use of descriptive statistics (means) and inferential measure (RÂ²) demonstrates appropriate analytical technique.
Trends and anomalies are linked to bid-rent theory, traffic corridors and transitional zones, showing clear interpretation.
All hypotheses are addressed, and anomalies (e.g. sites 4 and 7) are explored in relation to on-the-ground factors.
weaknesses: No formal significance testing (p-values, confidence intervals) is attempted to support RÂ² interpretations.
Analysis of Hypothesis 1 (vehicle volumes) lacks depth and justification for aggregating 40 sites into 10 points.
Discussion of EQS anomalies notes RÂ² = 0 but does not critically evaluate measurement reliability or bias.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work goes beyond basic description by integrating statistical analysis (RÂ²) and theory, linking patterns to the research question. While the absence of formal significance tests and some analytical justifications prevent reaching the very top band, the depth and coherence of interpretation justify a high mark (5 out of 8).

strengths: Conclusion clearly states which hypotheses were supported or rejected, citing RÂ² values directly.
All statements are fully supported by the preceding analysis, reflecting a logical, evidence-based summary.
weaknesses: The conclusion and analysis sections could be separated more cleanly with distinct headings for clarity and IA convention.
More reflection on broader geographic implications could enrich the concluding remarks.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is concise, directly tied to the analysis, and fully supported by evidence, satisfying the top-band descriptor for Criterion E.

strengths: Strengths and weaknesses of the investigation are clearly outlined (paired sampling, timing consistency vs. subjectivity, temporal variability).
Improvement suggestions (official noise data, fixed setback distance, simultaneous sampling) are relevant and linked to reducing bias.
Recommendations are detailed and include clear explanations of how each would improve reliability and validity.
weaknesses: Evaluation of how paired sampling specifically improved data reliability could be expanded.
Discussion could further explore the impact of uncontrolled variables (e.g. weather, pedestrian congestion).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation provides a comprehensive outline of methodological strengths and weaknesses and presents detailed, actionable suggestions with clear impact explanations. This meets the highest band descriptors for Criterion F.

strandSelections.reasoning: Three separate maps (national, district and site-scale trail map) include north arrows, legends and scale bars; they focus only on the study area, avoiding unnecessary detail. This demonstrates correct cartographic conventions and effective spatial representation, justifying the highest band.

The fieldwork question is clearly geographical, focused on spatial change in urban stress with distance from a CBD. The student explicitly links it to IB syllabus Option G and frames three measurable sub-hypotheses. Reference to bid-rent theory demonstrates a theoretical foundation that permits hypothesis testing. This satisfies the descriptor for the highest band.

The IA employs tables, scatter plots with trend lines/RÂ², annotated photographs and maps. These are appropriate and generally effective, but more advanced GIS or proportional symbols are absent, so they are not the âmost effective possibleâ. Upper marks of the 3-4 band are appropriate.

Techniques were implemented correctly (3-minute counts, average dB readings, paired EQS) and yielded workable datasets. Yet the student acknowledges subjectivity, differing groups and uncontrolled distance from roads, indicating reliability is moderate, not âhigh-qualityâ. This aligns with the mid-band descriptor.

The analysis applies descriptive statistics (means) and simple inferential statistic RÂ² to each scatter plot, which is appropriate but not exhaustive. No confidence limits or significance tests are attempted, so the work meets, but does not exceed, the 3-4 band.

The student links observed trends and anomalies to bid-rent theory, traffic corridors and transitional zones, demonstrating understanding beyond mere description. However, some explanations remain speculative and not all anomalies are fully explored, so the mid-band is most fitting.

The introduction locates Abidjan precisely and the maps identify the Plateau district, transect routes and individual sites at 100 m intervals. The specificity clearly shows that primary data collection is feasible and has in fact been carried out, matching the top-band requirement.

The evaluation section lists several strengths (paired collection, consistent timing) and weaknesses (subjectivity, time-of-day variability) with brief explanations of their effects, representing an âappropriate outlineâ rather than a detailed discussion, placing it in band 2.

Substantial primary data (40 sites on four transects for four variables) is provided and largely relevant to all hypotheses, enabling meaningful analysis, though the EQS scores appear inconsistent and some aggregation choices are unexplained. This fits âmost data relevant allowing partial analysisâ, warranting the upper mark of the 3-4 band.

The conclusion succinctly states which hypotheses were supported or rejected and cites RÂ² values, directly referencing the analysis. This is clear and wholly based on presented evidence, satisfying the top band for Criterion E.

All three hypotheses are addressed, yet the analysis of Hypothesis 1 cars is thin and aggregation from 40 to 10 sites is not justified. Gaps such as absence of triangulation or time-of-day controls remain, indicating analysis is solid but not complete, meriting the lower mark within the 3-4 band.

Methods (systematic sampling every 100 m, counts, decibel app, bipolar EQS) are described and linked to each hypothesis, showing clear relevance. However, explanation is brief; there is limited justification of why these methods best test bid-rent theory and no discussion of sampling size determination, so the description is adequate rather than fully comprehensive.

Figures have titles, labelled axes and legends; maps show scale and north. Minor lapses (e.g., some graph captions truncated, inconsistent unit formatting) are present but infrequent. This corresponds to âoccasional errorsâ, the top of the 3-4 band.

Improvement suggestions are relevantâuse official data, same observers, fixed distance from road, simultaneous collectionâand the student indicates how each would reduce bias. Explanations are concise but adequate, aligning with the mid-band descriptor.

annotations.comment: The fieldwork question precisely focuses on spatial change in urban stress and links clearly to bid-rent theory, facilitating hypothesis development.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment:
Consider specifying which urban stress indicators (e.g.
traffic flow vs. pedestrian counts) are primary to refine the questionâs scope further.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment:
The introduction provides solid demographic context but could deepen socio-economic insights (e.g.
income distribution, land use) to strengthen geographic framing.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Including the Decibel X screenshot is illustrative, but add a note on device calibration to strengthen data quality.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Aggregation method for EQS sub-indicators is unclear; justify how scores were combined to form a valid index.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Tables 2 and 3 are exhaustive, but inconsistent heading formats distract; standardise cell fonts and line weights.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: EQ score input table (Table 4) is crowded; consider splitting by transect or using a clearer grid.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The explanation of unexpected car volumes mentions arterial roads; consider mapping these corridors to visually support the claim.
The evaluation outlines strengths and weaknesses appropriately, but could expand on how paired sampling specifically improved reliability.
Figure 2 lacks standard cartographic elements (north arrow, scale bar); adding these will improve spatial clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Trail map clearly differentiates transects and follows mapping conventions, aiding site reproducibility.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Definition of urban stress is apt, but relying on a single EEA source limits depth; consider peer-reviewed studies.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Hypothesis 1 combines vehicles and pedestrians; separating them could yield clearer, more targeted analysis.
The validity of the bipolar EQS needs elaboration; explain how the scale was tested for consistency.
Bid-rent theory is well explained, but linking it directly to noise and EQS outcomes needs deeper theoretical grounding.
Citing Wikipedia for Figure 4 undermines academic rigour; replace with sourced GIS or peer-reviewed material.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Methodology mentions systematic sampling but omits justification of 40-site sample size; add rationale for representativeness.
Time-of-day variability is acknowledged; to reduce temporal bias, collect data simultaneously across transects.
Claiming Decibel X is âmore reliableâ needs evidence; include comparative accuracy data or calibration details.
Graph 1âs axes omit distance units (âmâ); adding units and tick increments will clarify data interpretation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment:
Noting an RÂ² of 0.252 is good but discuss its statistical significance (e.g.
p-value or confidence intervals) for robust analysis.
The pedestrian analysis (Graph 2) integrates theory well and demonstrates clear negative correlation.
The conclusion on hypothesis 1 is concise and draws directly from RÂ² data, effectively fulfilling Criterion E.
Noise analysis aptly links RÂ² to built environment factors; add caveat on RÂ² limitations for non-linear patterns.
Anomalies at sites 4 and 7 are well explored; referencing specific junction layouts could strengthen context.
Environmental quality discussion notes RÂ² = 0 but lacks critique of EQS scale reliability; evaluate potential measurement bias.
Separate the analysis and conclusion sections with clear headings to enhance structure and meet IA conventions.
Recommending a fixed 10 m setback for noise readings is an excellent, actionable improvement to reduce measurement bias.

Suggest integrating specific online datasets (e.g.
official noise maps) to replace subjective EQS data for greater objectivity.
